IS DENIED AS UNTIMELY AND FOR FAILURE TO MEET AND CONFER. "UNLESS OTHERWISE ORDERED OR SPECIFICALLY PROVIDED BY LAW, ALL MOVING AND SUPPORTING PAPERS SHALL BE SERVED AND FILED AT LEAST 16 COURT DAYS BEFORE THE HEARING." CAL. CIV. PROC. CODE 1005. SERVICE OF PAPERS VIA E-SERVICE EXTENDS "ANY RESPONSE . . . BY TWO COURT DAYS." CAL. CIV. PROC. CODE 1010.6(A)(4); SEE ALSO CAL. R. CT. 2.251(H)(2). MORE, PLAINTIFF'S COUNSEL FAILED TO MEET AND CONFER WITH DEFENDANT, REPRESENTING HIMSELF, BEFORE FILING THIS MOTION. "THE DISCOVERY ACT REQUIRES THAT, PRIOR TO THE INITIATION OF A MOTION TO COMPEL, THE MOVING PARTY DECLARE THAT HE OR SHE HAS MADE A SERIOUS ATTEMPT TO OBTAIN AN INFORMAL RESOLUTION OF EACH ISSUE." Off Calendar Jan-22-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances: The Court adopted its tentative ruling as follows: PLAINTIFF AARON DRENBERG'S Motion For Entry Of Default Against Defendant James K. Song, Jr. Or Alternatively To Compel, And For Sanctions. is denied as untimely and for failure to meet and confer. "Unless otherwise ordered or specifically provided by law, all moving and supporting papers shall be served and filed at least 16 court days before the hearing." Cal. Civ. Proc. Code 1005. Service of papers via e-service extends "any response . . . by two court days." Cal. Civ. Proc. Code 1010.6(a)(4); see also Cal. R. Ct. 2.251(h)(2). More, Plaintiff's counsel failed to meet and confer with Defendant, representing himself, before filing this motion. "The Discovery Act requires that, prior to the initiation of a motion to compel, the moving party declare that he or she has made a serious attempt to obtain an informal resolution of each issue." Townsend v. Superior Court, 61 Cal. App. 4th 1431, 1435. Prevailing party is require to prepare a proposed order. Judge Pro Tem Tom Cohen, Clerk K. Liu, not reported. (D302/JPT)

Off Calendar Jan-11-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Adam Engel, Esq. 212-665-8095 the Engel Law Group for Defendant through Court Call.
No other Appearance.

RULING - The matter is Uncontested. Defendant informs the Court Counsel for opposing side contests the proposed order. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT JAMES SONG, JR.'s MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendant James Song, Jr.'s motion to set default and vacate default judgment is GRANTED. Plaintiff Aaron Drenberg failed to show that the address on which substitute service was purportedly effected was the "dwelling house or usual place of abode" of Song. Although, Drenberg has submitted evidence showing that Song stayed at his parents' house, when in the US, that evidence does not refute Song's declaration that he did not reside at the address at the relevant time. (Song Decl. 19-23). The proof of service filed does not purport to show that substitute service was made at the usual mailing address of Song. The default and default judgment are vacated pursuant to CCP 473(d). (See Hearn v. Howard (2009) 177 Cal.App.4th 1193, 1200, "[b]ecause the law favors disposing of cases on their merits, 'any doubts in applying section 473' must be resolved in favor of the party seeking relief from default.") Song shall file and serve a responsive pleading within 20 days.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Notice Of Motion To Quash Service Of Summons
Off Calendar Mar-26-2018 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Alexandra Arneri, Esq. of Wood Robbins LLP, T:415.247.7900, for defendant/moving party;
Mark Pettinari, Esq. of the Law Offices of Mark L. Pettinari, T:415.233.3346, for plaintiff/opposing party.

RULING: The tentative ruling was contested and argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopts the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT CATHERINE SONG's Motion To Quash Service Of Summons is GRANTED. Plaintiff Aaron Drenberg fails to show that this court may exercise specific jurisdiction over Ms. Song under the effects test. (Burdick v. Superior Court (2015) 233 Cal.App.4th 8.) There is no evidence that Ms. Song expressly aimed her alleged tortious conduct at California. (Burdick, 233 Cal.App.4th at 20.) The only evidence that Mr. Drenberg proffers is his declaration and a declaration from his counsel. That evidence fails to show that Ms. Song specifically directed her comments toward California. Mr. Drenberg's request to conduct jurisdictional discovery is denied since Mr. Drenberg has not identified any facts that he might discover that would suffice for specific jurisdiction over Ms. Song.

Counsel for Ms. Song is to prepare a proposed order and a proposed judgment which conform to the Court's ruling and submit them in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: G. Gonzales; Reporter: Laura Reding, CSR 711, email: lreding@sftc.org.
